coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent nanopaper-based microcuvette for iodide detection in seawater
coredata.dc:creator: Zor, Erhan
Alpaydin, Sabri
Arici, Aylin
Saglam, Muhammed Esad
Bingol, Haluk
coredata.dc:description: Nanopaper defined as a sheet made of cellulose nanofibers has recently attracted a growing interest in paper-based flexible and transparent sustainable transistors, electronics, devices and sensors. Use of bacterial cellulose nanopaper, particularly in optical sensing platforms, is becoming increasingly popular owing to its numerous desirable properties. Herein, we propose a carbon quantum dots-embedded paper-based sensor for selective iodide sensing. To this aim, the nanopaper was obtained by an environment-friendly approach using bacterial cellulose made of nanofibers (the diameter of nanofibers was observed as 40±10nm), which were produced by A. xylinum. Then, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were embedded into nanopaper to gain photoluminescence properties. The obtained photoluminescent nanopaper was cut into circular pieces for easily handling, or rectangular pieces for use in a disposable wax-printed paper-based microcuvette which could be inserted in a common spectrofluorometer. Finally, the nanopaper pieces were used for anion sensing and we obtained a selective detection of iodide in seawater (as a model real sample media). Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values were respectively calculated as 48 and 144μM (6.1 and 18.3ppm) for microcuvette application in seawater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanopaper
Bacterial cellulose
Carbon quantum dots
Photoluminescent sensor
Iodide sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Detection of iodide (I−) in solution and nanopaper
Lifetime and the stability of the produced bacterial cellulose nanopaper
Performance of the N-CQDs as a sensing material

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Scientific Research Projects of Necmettin Erbakan University (project 161310004) for support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the experimental procedure.
Schematic representation of the preparation of microcuvette modulation.
PL spectra of N-CQDs. The insets are the photographs of the aqueous solution of N-CQDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right) (a). FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs (b).
XPS survey spectrum (a), C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c) and O1s spectra of N-CQDs prepared from citric acid and HMDA (d).
TEM images of N-CQDs at different magnifications at 20nm (a) and at 5nm (a′). SEM images of nanopaper at different magnifications at 1μm (b) and at 200nm (b′), respectively.

PL spectra of the N-CQDs in the presence of different anions of 5.0mM in aqueous solution (a). The changes in PL spectra of the N-CQDs solution at different concentrations of I− ions in pure water (b) and the corresponding calibration curve (c).

The color change in the presence of the tested anion species (5.0mM of fluoride, chloride, bromide, iodide, acetate, nitrate and sulfate) (a). The N-CQDs-embedded nanopapers after treatment with I− to examine the influence of sample concentration (25μM–25mM) on the photoluminescence signal under daylight (b) and under UV light (c).
The PL spectra of the microcuvette-modulated photoluminescent nanopaper at different concentrations of I− in seawater (a). The corresponding calibration graph (b).
The analytical characteristics of the sensor based on iodide detection in different media.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent nanopaper-based microcuvette for iodide detection in seawater

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science Education, A. K. Education Faculty, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry Education, A. K. Education Faculty, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Institute of Science, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanopaper defined as a sheet made of cellulose nanofibers has recently attracted a growing interest in paper-based flexible and transparent sustainable transistors, electronics, devices and sensors. Use of bacterial cellulose nanopaper, particularly in optical sensing platforms, is becoming increasingly popular owing to its numerous desirable properties. Herein, we propose a carbon quantum dots-embedded paper-based sensor for selective iodide sensing. To this aim, the nanopaper was obtained by an environment-friendly approach using bacterial cellulose made of nanofibers (the diameter of nanofibers was observed as 40±10nm), which were produced by A. xylinum. Then, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were embedded into nanopaper to gain photoluminescence properties. The obtained photoluminescent nanopaper was cut into circular pieces for easily handling, or rectangular pieces for use in a disposable wax-printed paper-based microcuvette which could be inserted in a common spectrofluorometer. Finally, the nanopaper pieces were used for anion sensing and we obtained a selective detection of iodide in seawater (as a model real sample media). Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values were respectively calculated as 48 and 144μM (6.1 and 18.3ppm) for microcuvette application in seawater.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have the promising potential to revolutionize simple and disposable sensors. In the last decade, nanomaterial-incorporated paper-based (bio)sensors have provided new opportunities and directions for the development of precise and sensitive analytical devices. With the recent advances in flexible and transparent sensor (nano)technology, nanopaper-based sensors have attracted great attention of researchers [1]. Common paper is made of cellulose fibers with an average diameter of ∼25μm, which induces significant light scattering that results in an opaque substrate [2]. However, nanopaper is a transparent film due to the network-forming of nanocellulose fibers which are several micrometers long with a diameter below 50nm [3]. In addition, nanopaper is capable of adsorbing various kinds of ions, such as heavy metal ions or cationic dyes, via electrostatic interaction between functional groups and ions [4].

A rising concept of adopting nanopaper-based platforms has stirred excitement in anticipation of sustainable flexible and transparent transistors [5], optoelectronics [6], devices [7], electrochromics [8] and sensors [1,9–11]. A range of (nano)materials can be deposited into the nanopaper, and it may exhibit selective detection capability for different kind of ions. Morales-Narváez et al. have evaluated plasmonic (silver nanoparticles and gold nanoparticles) and photoluminescent (CdSe@ZnS quantum dots) nanoparticles incorporated-nanopaper as sensing platforms [1]. Besides these nanoparticles, carbon-based dots (CDs) are ambitious candidates to develop disposable nanopaper-based photoluminescent platforms for practical use owing to their advantages such as their superior fluorescence properties, non-toxic, biocompatible, broad chemical-modification, inexpensive to produce and easy to handle capabilities [12,13]. The combination of printable/embeddability feature on solid platforms and photoluminescent characteristics of CDs with the advantages of nanopaper can endow new opportunities for optical sensor assay fabrication. Nanopaper-based platforms have been scarcely used for optical (bio)sensing applications, however no published study exists for CDs embedded photoluminescent sensors to date. Hence, we sought to fabricate, and test a simple and disposable nanopaper-based sensor capable of detecting iodide (I−) anion that not only plays critical roles in biologically and inorganically mediated oxidation/reduction reactions in the marine environment [14] but also have essential roles in neurological activity and thyroid gland function [15,16]. Thus, the determination of iodide is critically important in both the life and environmental sciences.

Given the aforementioned context, we combined the advantages of nanopaper and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for the development of a disposable paper-based sensor. We developed single use, robust and cost-effective wax-printed cuvettes, into which nanopaper (rectangular pieces) could be easily inserted, and we evaluated the cuvettes for the practical use in spectrofluorometer. By inserting rectangular pieces into wax-printed cuvettes, we could achieve to minimize handling of the sample without exposure to it and decrease the sample volume. Thus, we made device safer and more convenient for small volume sample analysis. As a paper-based assay, this CQDs-embedded nanopaper-based sensor is simple to manipulate and rapid, which endows a fast readout in less than 5min that can be a useful model to develop new point-of-care (POC) devices.

Hexamethylenediamine (HMDA), citric acid, glucose, (NH4)2SO4, KH2PO4 MgSO4·7H2O, NaOH, H2O2 and sodium salts of the tested anions (fluoride, chloride, bromide, iodide, acetate, nitrate and sulfate) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using Tecnai G2-F30, FEI. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs were taken by a ZEISS EVO LS10 at accelerating voltage of 20kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Perkin Elmer 100 FTIR in the 550–4000cm−1 range in ambient air at room temperature. Photoluminescence (PL) experiments were recorded with PTI QuantaMaster™ 40 steady state spectrofluorometer system (QM40) using a high intensity continuous Xe lamp. A ColorQube 8580 wax printer was used to fabricate the disposable cuvettes. A teflon-lined autoclave was used for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. Ultrapure water was prepared by a Milli-Q system (Millipore). Seawater was obtained from seaside of Antalya.

Bacterial cellulose nanopaper was fabricated using Acetobacter xylinum bacteria in 50g glucose, 5g yeast extract, 5g (NH4)2SO4, 4g KH2PO4 and 0.1g MgSO4·7H2O in 1L of water for two weeks at 28°C [1]. Then, the obtained 3–4 mm-thick unpurified wet mat containing cellulose nanofibers, bacteria and the other impurities was purified by treating with 5wt% NaOH for 24h at room temperature, followed by 1wt% NaOH/0.2wt% H2O2 for 1h at 80°C. After purification step, the resultant wet mat fully made from bacterial cellulose nanofibers was washed with distilled water to remove the chemicals and to obtain a transparent nanopaper (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were fabricated by a facile and one-pot hydrothermal method using HMDA and citric acid [17]. To prepare N-CQDs, 0.5g citric acid was mixed with 55mg HMDA and the mixture was heated to 240°C for 5h using a teflon-lined autoclave. Subsequently, the color of the melting mixture changed from colorless to pale brown. The resultant N-CQDs solution was purified within the dialysis bag (MWCO: 2kDa) [18]. PL spectra of the purified N-CQDs were given in Fig. 1 a.

For sensing experiments, N-CQDs were embedded into nanopaper by immersing nanopaper into solution of N-CQDs. Then, the water-swollen nanopaper was dried in drying oven at room temperature. The N-CQDs-embedded nanopaper was cut into circular and rectangular pieces to prepare the desired disposable photoluminescent sensors, and they were stored in dark conditions at 4°C before use. Afterwards, the prepared transparent and blue photoluminescent nanopapers were treated with the solution of sodium salts of anions (fluoride, chloride, bromide, iodide, acetate, nitrate and sulfate) by syringing on them. For fabrication of disposable and optically-transparent cuvettes, we used a wax printing machine to create the desired layouts onto common paper. For this aim, cuvette patterns were drawn by CorelDRAW-X6 and printed onto common paper. Then, the paper was folded along the wax-printed two-dimensional layouts to make it more favorable for use in sample holder of a common spectrofluorometer. Finally, the treated photoluminescent nanopaper was inserted into the fabricated two-dimensional cuvettes and used for sensing applications. The experimental procedure is schematized in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of iodide (I−) in solution and nanopaper
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of I− in solution phase and nanopaper was performed at room temperature. Stock solution of iodide was prepared as 0.1M by dissolving NaI powder in 100.0mL of water. Working standard solutions were prepared by serial dilutions from the stock solution. In a typical experiment in solution phase, a 10mL of as-synthesized N-CQD solution was diluted to a total volume of 100mL sample (pure water, tap water or seawater). Then, the desired final concentration of I− was adjusted with stock solution by keeping the final volume of solution at 3mL. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the resulting samples were recorded. For the practical applications with nanopaper (spots and microcuvvettes), an adjusted volume of working standard solutions (around 100μL) was added on nanopapers and they were held for drying at room temperature. Then, PL spectra of nanopapers were recorded to observe photoluminescence change.

The characterization of N-CQDs was performed by fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) techniques. The characterization of nanopaper was carried out by scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

The photoluminescence properties of N-CQDs were first examined. Fig. 1a shows the photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission profile of N-CQDs solution. As seen, a PL emission peak centered at 434nm was obtained when the excitation wavelength was set at 369nm. The photograph in the inset of Fig. 1a shows N-CQDs solution taken under daylight and 365nm UV irradiation. As seen, N-CQDs solution exhibits a bright blue color which confirms the strong PL profile of N-CQDs.

The FT-IR spectrum of the N-CQDs (Fig. 1b) shows the absorption bands at around 3440, 3140, 2936, 1708 and 1550cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CH, CO, and CC, respectively. The peak at 1395cm−1 can be attributed to the COO− group. In addition, the two typical peaks at 1634 and 1232cm−1 correspond to stretching modes of CN and CN, respectively. The peaks at 1000–1200cm−1 are ascribed to CO and CN stretching and OH bending vibrations [19,20].

XPS technique was further performed for surface characterization N-CQDs possessing O and N-function groups (see Fig. 2 ). C1s, N1s and O1s signals were observed at ∼285, 401 and 531eV for N-CQDs (Fig. 2a), respectively. Due to the existence of N-functional groups, CQDs can display a positively charged surface. This feature can be beneficial for the sensing of anions because of the strong electrostatic interaction. Similar to the previous reports [16,21], CC, CC, CN, CO, CO/CN at 283.8, 284.4, 285.0, 286.1, and 288.1eV were observed in the expanded C1s XPS spectrum as shown Fig. 2b. N1s was deconvoluted into four sub-peaks given in Fig. 2c: ∼399.3, 400.3, 401.1, 401.9eV which were respectively ascribed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, graphitic-like N [22] and NH types [23]. The O1s band displayed three major deconvoluted peaks at 529.9, 532.0, and 533.4eV, corresponding to the CO (ketonic carbonyl groups), OCO groups, and COH (phenol and alcohol groups), see Fig. 2d [24]. These results support the successfully doping of nitrogen and are consistent with FT-IR results.

Fig. 3 a and a′ show the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs of the N-CQDs at different magnifications, verifying the production of uniform N-CQDs with a diameter of ∼10nm. Fig. 3b and 3b′ show SEM micrographs of the prepared nanopaper at low and high magnifications. The diameter of nanofibers were measured as 40±10nm on SEM micrographs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lifetime and the stability of the produced bacterial cellulose nanopaper
ce:section.ce:para: Lifetime and the stability of sensors are important issues for practical use [11]. In particular, by transferring the used materials (quantum dots/dyes) onto solid platforms, the photoluminescence intensity may decrease. This drawback property was overcome by embedding carbon dots, which were stable not only in solution but also on solid phase. The stability of the fabricated carbon quantum dots-embedded nanopapers was examined by storing them not only in a sealed brown bottle at 4°C but also by settling them in laboratory cabinet for six months. The photoluminescence intensity was followed by both naked eye under UV-irradiation and recording PL spectrum. No significant change in PL spectra was observed during one month, whereas a ∼30% decrease occurred in PL intensity after three months and a ∼50% after six months. Aforementioned results show the long lifetime of the fabricated carbon quantum dots-embedded nanopaper even at ambient temperature for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the N-CQDs as a sensing material
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing capability of N-CQDs was initially determined in solution phase by following PL intensity. The solutions of fluoride, chloride, bromide, iodide, acetate, nitrate and sulfate ions were spiked into the solution of N-CQDs (Fig. 4 a). The addition of I− led to a quench in the PL intensity, whereas no change was observed for the other tested ions as shown in Fig. 4b. Wang et al. have reported that the photoluminescence of carbon dots can be efficiently quenched by both the electron acceptor and the electron donor and these interesting photoinduced electron transfer features may offer new beneficial facilities in potential use of carbon dots for light energy conversion and related applications [25]. This suggests the possibility of utilizing the carbon-based dots for the anion sensing [16,26]. Recently, Zhang et al. have demonstrated that the excited oxidation state of N-CQDs can react with I− to form I3− under light excitation, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs [27]. Apart from the quenching principle, it is also reasonable to remark that although the selectivity of N-CQDs toward I− has been experimentally demonstrated, the precise reason(s) is not clear. Nevertheless, the selectivity can be elucidated by the following rationale: (i) the positively charged N-CQDs surfaces allowing strong electrostatic interactions with I−. Zhang et al. have investigated the importance of N-doping and reported this process can increase the sensitivity of CQDs for I− detection because the negatively charged CQDs surfaces hinder the electrostatic interactions. (ii) Soft Lewis base characteristics of I−, which is one of the largest monatomic electron-rich atoms. Owing to this feature, I− can display a spatial shape change in the case of its interaction with hard Lewis acids. This inherent characteristics makes I− more convenient than harder Lewis anion bases in matching the spatial configuration of self-trapped excitons at N-CQDs [16]. The aforementioned behavior endows strong interactions between I− and N-CQDs which results in a good selectivity towards I−.

Taking into account the corresponding calibration curve (Fig. 4c) for each aqueous media, limit of detection (LOD, i.e., the interpolation of the mean of the blank signal plus 3 times its standard deviation into the fitting curve) and limit of quantification (LOQ, i.e., the interpolation of the mean of the blank signal plus 10 times its standard deviation into the fitting curve) were respectively calculated as 29 and 87μM (3.7 and 11ppm) in pure water and the analytical characteristics of the sensor based on iodide determination were tabulated in Table 1 .

Aiming at investigating the robustness and the potential utility of N-CQDs for real sample analysis, we carried out a series of PL measurements in tap water and seawater (as matrixes) by spiking different concentrations of I−, and the results were shown in Fig. S2a-d. The emission peak at 434nm gradually decreased with the increasing amount of I−. Taking into account the corresponding calibration curves (see Fig. S2b and S2d) for each aqueous media, LODs and LOQs were calculated as 37 and 111μM (4.7 and 14.1ppm), 41 and 123μM (5.2 and 15.6ppm) in tap water and seawater, respectively, which are comparable to the reported fluorescence method employing carbon quantum dots [16] and fluorescent Ce(III) species [28]. Compared to the other detection methods such as the electrochemical [15] (LOD is 0.6μM), laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry [29] (LOD is 0.5nM), colorimetric [30] (LOD is 10nM) methods, although the detection limit of I− in this work is higher, it has advantages over other methods such as not only its simplicity in analytical operation but also inexpensive and eco-friendly nature of N-CQDs. Moreover, the proposed method covers the direct determination of I− without the need of any indicator such as metal ions which would make the system more complex as well as increasing uncertainty in understanding the whole process.

Bearing in mind the selectivity performance of N-CQDs, we sought to develop a simple disposable paper-based I− selective sensor for practical applications. Within this respect, as previously mentioned, we developed a selective sensor for I− not only in the solution phase but also on solid platform by using nanopaper. As proof-of-concept, the performance of N-CQDs embedded bacterial nanopapers was evaluated by measuring the PL change in the presence of the tested anions. For this aim, N-CQDs embedded bacterial nanopapers were cut in circular pieces and treated with the tested anions by syringing. Fig. S3 illustrates the photographs of blank nanopaper and N-CQDs embedded nanopapers under daylight and UV light, which shows that blank nanopaper is optically transparent whereas N-CQDs emit blue color under UV light. As clearly seen in Fig. 5 a, the blue emission of N-CQDs embedded bacterial nanopaper remarkably quenched only in the presence of I− whereas almost no change was observed for the other tested anions, demonstrating that N-CQDs embedded bacterial nanopaper could be used as an effective disposable sensor for I−. As observed solution phase experiments, this quenching behavior stemmed from the photoinduced electron transfer between N-CQDs which were embedded into nanopaper and I− ions in the sample. We further assessed our N-CQDs embedded transparent bacterial nanopapers to examine the influence of sample concentration of I− on the PL signal. As easily seen in Fig. 5b and 5c by naked eye, PL of N-CQDs embedded bacterial nanopapers was gradually decreased upon addition of increasing amounts of I− (25μM–25mM). After 0.5mM of I− addition, no change was observed in photoluminescence intensity, indicating the saturation of the sensing response.

The interesting issue to be addressed was whether the selective sensing capability of N-CQDs would be examined in a common spectrofluorometer by using a disposable microcuvette. To this aim, the microcuvette modulation for fluorometric measurements at solid phase was performed at the same concentration range, see Scheme 2 . Our initial design criteria for the microcuvettes was the angle of light source and non-photoluminescent disposable sensor, and thus control of the overall emission upon interaction of N-CQDs and I−. As aforementioned, wax printed regular papers were used as hydrophobic microcuvettes which were non-photoluminescent and could be easily inserted into holder of spectrofluorometer with a precise angle. In our sensing system, because of the requirement of entirely wetting of the nanopaper, an adjusted volume of sample (around 100μL) was required to assure efficiently capturing of analyte which led to PL quench in confined aqueous solution by nanopaper. After this process, the treated nanopaper was dried at room temperature and finally inserted into the microcuvette to perform PL measurements using spectrofluorometer.

Fig. 6 a shows the emission profile of the microcuvette-modulated photoluminescent nanopaper as a function of I− concentration which exhibits a linear calibration curve (Fig. 6b) for increasing concentration of I− in seawater. The operational range was between 0.025 and 25mM. The sensitivity was determined as 933.62M−1. Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) values were calculated as 48 and 144μM (6.1 and 18.3ppm). Also, from a scientific point of view, the developed nanopaper-based sensor for the direct determination of iodide has lower LOD value among the single-use sensors. For example, Khodari et al. have developed disposable screen-printed electrodes for amperometric determination of iodide ions with the lowest iodide concentration measured as 100μM [14].

We developed a simple and rapid approach for sensitive and selective optical sensing of I− by taking advantage of the proposed nanopaper-based sensor. Aiming at researching the analytical performance of the engineered sensor under optimal conditions, wax-printed microcuvettes were developed and applied for selective anion detection. Qualitative analyses were performed by simply treating with iodide and observing (with the naked eye) the generation of a change in photoluminescence of N-CQDs-embedded nanopaper under UV light irritation. The blue photoluminescence of N-CQDs-embedded transparent nanopaper was quenched only in the presence of I−, whereas no change was observed for the other tested anions which demonstrated that N-CQDs embedded nanopaper could be used as an effective disposable sensor for I− anion. Quantitative measurements could be taken using a common spectrofluorometer, whereby the intensity of the photoluminescence was quantified according to a normalized scale. Overall, the results proved that the produced photoluminescent N-CQDs embedded nanopaper was successfully applied in the detection of I−, even in a complex medium such as real sample of tap water and seawater without any sample treatment. Considering the performance of the prepared sensor, we can conclude that the proposed optical sensor enables a simple and fast way for the direct determination of I− without the need of any indicator. In addition, the proposed sensing strategy provides the analysis of much lower sample volumes (∼100μL) than previously reported approaches. These findings may open the way to development of novel and advantageous tests based on photoluminescent nanopaper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Scientific Research Projects of Necmettin Erbakan University (project 161310004) for support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.07.208.
Experimental details such as production steps of nanopaper and the changes in PL spectra of the N-CQDs solution for different aqueous media are included.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanopaper as an optical sensing platform
Transparent nanopaper with tailored optical properties
Nitrate removal from water using a nanopaper ion-exchanger
Highly transparent and flexible nanopaper transistors
Extreme light management in mesoporous wood cellulose paper for optoelectronics
Chemical modification of cellulose nanofibers for the production of highly thermal resistant and optically transparent nanopaper for paper devices
Foldable electrochromics enabled by nanopaper transfer method
Modulation of population density and size of silver nanoparticles embedded in bacterial cellulose via ammonia exposure: visual detection of volatile compounds in a piece of plasmonic nanopaper
Green in-situ synthesized silver nanoparticles embedded in bacterial cellulose nanopaper as a bionanocomposite plasmonic sensor
Tunable photoluminescence across the entire visible spectrum from carbon dots excited by white light
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Screen-Printed electrodes for amperometric determination of iodide
Graphene sheet-starch platform based on the groove recognition for the sensitive and highly selective determination of iodide in seafood samples
Determination of iodide via direct fluorescence quenching at nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot fluorophores
Formation mechanism and optimization of highly luminescent N-doped graphene quantum dots
Glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots as selective fluorescent probes for phosphate-containing metabolites

Facile Synthesis of N-Doped Carbon Dots as a New Matrix for Detection of Hydroxy-Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons by Negative-Ion Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight Mass Spectrometry
Large-scale synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots and their phosphorescence properties in a polyurethane matrix
Nitrogen-doped{,} carbon-rich{,} highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
Elemental superdoping of graphene and carbon nanotubes
Silver nanoparticles/N-doped carbon-dots nanocomposites derived from siraitia grosvenorii and its logic gate and surface-enhanced raman scattering characteristics
Deciphering a nanocarbon-Based artificial peroxidase: chemical identification of the catalytically active and substrate-binding sites on graphene quantum dots
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Fluorescence determination of nitrite in water using prawn-Shell derived nitrogen-Doped carbon nanodots as fluorophores
A fluorescent quenching performance enhancing principle for carbon nanodot-sensitized aqueous solar cells
A portable multi-syringe flow system for spectrofluorimetric determination of iodide in seawater
Gold-nanoparticles-modified cellulose membrane coupled with laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry for detection of iodide in urine
A highly selective and sensitive colorimetric sensor for iodide detection based on anti-aggregation of gold nanoparticles

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Erhan Zor received his PhD in chemistry in 2016. His current research interest focuses on (bio)sensor applications of nanomaterials. He is the author of more than 20 articles based on his scientific studies.

Dr. Sabri Alpaydin obtained his PhD in chemistry from Selcuk University in 1999. He is an associated professor at Necmettin Erbakan University. The activities of his research include the nanocomposite materials, the surface functionalization and the development of low-cost nanostructure fabrication methods in the areas of membrane technology.
Aylin Arici is a master student in nanoscience and nanoengineering at Necmettin Erbakan University. Her research interest includes micro/nanofibers for sensor applications.

Muhammed Esad Saglam is a master student in chemistry at Necmettin Erbakan University. He has recently focused on interdisciplinary topics such as surface modification, characterization of composite materials and sensor applications.

Dr. Haluk Bingol is an associated professor at Necmettin Erbakan University. He received his PhD in Chemistry in 2007. His research activities include the chemical synthesis of graphene related materials and their application in (bio)sensors. He is author of more than 30 publications in the sensor and nanotechnology field in refereed scholarly journals.
